title-group.article-title: Multisession fNIRS-EEG data of Post-Stroke Motor Recovery. Recordings During Intact and Paretic Hand Movements

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/03f9nc143grid.454320.40000 0004 0555 3608Center for Bio- and Medical Technologies, Skolkovo Institute of Science and Technology, Moscow, Russia

To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Accurate diagnosis and monitoring of recovery after stroke are critical for effective motor rehabilitation. As stroke is inherently associated with impaired cerebral blood flow, functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) provides a valuable tool for assessing hemodynamic changes in the brain. When combined with electroencephalography (EEG), this multimodal approach can provide complementary insights into neural and vascular responses during recovery. However, longitudinal datasets combining fNIRS and EEG in stroke populations remain limited. The current article presents an open access dataset with simultaneous fNIRS and EEG recordings from 16 post-stroke patients over 84 rehabilitation sessions. Participants performed motor tasks with both paretic and intact hands. The dataset includes raw and processed signals, clinical scores (ARAT, Fugl-Meyer) and patient demographics. This resource supports research into stroke recovery, development of neurorehabilitation strategies and fNIRS-based brain computer interfaces (BCI).

institution-wrap.institution: Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation (Minobrnauka)

sec.title: Background & Summary
sec.p: Stroke remains a leading cause of long-term disability worldwide, with motor impairments significantly affecting quality of life1,2. Understanding neural mechanisms underlying motor recovery is crucial for developing effective rehabilitation strategies. Since stroke fundamentally involves cerebral blood supply disruption, hemodynamic assessment methods may bring new insights. Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) is a promising non-invasive technique for cortical hemodynamics capturing at depths up to 4 cm from the scalp. Near-infrared light (760–850 nm) measurements can be converted into estimations of oxygenated (HbO) and deoxygenated (HbR) hemoglobin3. Compared to functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), fNIRS offers advantages including portability, procedural simplicity, and lower cost, facilitating longitudinal monitoring of cortical activity within a rehabilitation4,5.

Despite these advantages of fNIRS, longitudinal studies tracking cortical hemodynamics across multiple trials of post-stroke rehabilitation remain rare. Repeated fNIRS measurements during rehabilitation are critical for mapping cortical activation to functional recovery, establishing robust neural biomarkers, and supporting adaptive therapy6.

Electroencephalography (EEG) is becoming more widely used in post-stroke prognosis and rehabilitation, but significant gaps remain in the understanding of the underlying neurophysiological mechanisms. For example, the precise nature of sensorimotor rhythm reactivity and motor cortical potentials during movement execution in post-stroke subjects remains poorly characterised, limiting the clinical and translational use of these markers for diagnosis and brain computer interface (BCI) -based rehabilitation.

Simultaneous acquisition of EEG and fNIRS provides complementary information: EEG captures fast neural dynamics, while fNIRS reflects slower cortical hemodynamic responses7–9. Combined with a longitudinal design, these data allow a tracking of recovery-related dynamics over time, providing a basis for the identification of predictive biomarkers and the development of personalized rehabilitation strategies. As the experimental paradigm included recordings during movements of both the paretic and intact hands, the dataset captures evolving interhemispheric interactions and compensatory mechanisms throughout recovery. The potential results from the dataset analysis provide a basis for further development of rehabilitation technologies, such as adaptive closed-loop neurorehabilitation systems that use healthy hand induced activity for training or integrate both electrical and hemodynamic signals for BCI control10–14.

sec.title: Study subjects
sec.p: Sixteen patients with upper limb hemiparesis (age range: 42–71 years, mean age ± std: 57.2 ± 9.68 years; 6 females, all right-handed) were recruited for this study. The cohort comprised 13 patients in the subacute recovery phase (<6 months post-stroke) and 3 patients in the chronic recovery phase (>6 months but <1 year post-stroke). Stroke etiology was classified as ischemic (N = 13) or hemorrhagic (N = 3). The stroke localization was confirmed by CT or MRI. Cognitive and language functions were preserved. All participants presented with moderate disability (modified Rankin Scale score of 3).

Motor and somatosensory functions were assessed using the Action Research Arm Test (ARAT) and the Fugl-Meyer Assessment (FMA). The FMA covered multiple domains: upper extremity motor function (FMA-UE; 0–66 points), sensory function including light touch and proprioception (FMA-S; 0–12 points), passive joint range of motion (0–24 points), and joint pain (0–24 points), yielding a maximum total score of 126 points. Detailed baseline and post-rehabilitation scores for all participants are presented in Table 1.Table 1
Patient characteristics including age, affected limb, clinical assessment scores, and lesion localization.
IDSexAgeParalyzed handLesion LocalizationFugl-Meyer UE beforeFugl-Meyer UE Sensory beforeARAT beforeFugl-Meyer UE afterFugl-Meyer UE Sensory afterARAT after
L_01M66leftrMCA1660220602
R_02M48rightlMCA25589305811
R_03M70rightlMCA526027566036
L_04F66leftVBS406019426026
L_05F71leftVBS376021416034
L_06M60leftrMCA66009600
R_07M47rightlCH506040536043
L_08M49leftrCH854010543
L_09M57leftrMCA366028386032
R_10M64rightlMCA575851595853
L_11M52leftSA1060010600
L_12F70leftVBS2358028580
R_13F55rightlMCA——34——46
L_14M45leftrCH——0——0
L_15F54leftrMCA356015406015
R_16F42rightlMCA——32——36
Lesion locations are abbreviated as follows: right/left middle cerebral artery (r/l MCA), vertebrobasilar system (VBS), subarachnoid hemorrhage (SA), right/left cerebellar hemisphere (r/l CH).

All participants provided written informed consent after receiving a comprehensive explanation of the experimental procedures. The study was conducted in accordance with the ethical standards established in the Declaration of Helsinki. The research protocol was approved by the Local Ethics Committee of the Skolkovo Institute of Science and Technology (protocol № 10 dated May 18, 2023). The ID of research in ClinicalTrials.gov is NCT06806397. Patient data were anonymized and depersonalized according to applicable regulations.

sec.title: Experimental procedure
sec.p: A stimulus-response task (SRT) was used for testing. Participants were seated at a standardized testing table with both hands placed in a custom-designed box, palms oriented down. Two response buttons were positioned above the participants’ hands, designed to be pressed through wrist and finger extension. Each button was paired with a light-emitting diode (LED) positioned directly above it. The participant was instructed to respond to each LED flash by pressing the corresponding button.

Each experimental session consisted of several individual runs in which participants performed the SRT with a target hand, as instructed before each run. For example, if the right hand was assigned as the target, the flashing of the LED above the right button (target stimulus) required the participant to press the right button while ignoring any flashing of the LED corresponding to the left hand (non-target stimulus). Each session consisted of at least three runs for each hand, with target assignments randomized for each run and varying across participants.

Each run included 12 light flashes (six per LED) presented in a pseudo-random order. Stimulus duration was 200 ms, with intervals between flashes ranging from 9 to 11 seconds (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
(Left) Schematic of the experimental procedure using the left run as an example. The subject had to respond only to the left flashing by pressing the corresponding button, while the right flashing was a non-target stimulus and had to be ignored. (Right) View of the experimental setup and the subject.

Throughout the study period, all participants underwent a standardized two-week inpatient rehabilitation program at the Federal Center for Brain and Neurotechnology (FMBA, Russia). While the rehabilitation interventions were heterogeneous across the cohort, all protocols followed established clinical guidelines. Each patient participated in a series of experimental sessions (minimum: 3; maximum: 6) performed on non-consecutive days.
Post-rehabilitation motor function was assessed using the FMA-UE and ARAT scales to analyze dynamics after the intervention period.

sec.title: Signal recording
sec.p: The data were collected using NIRSport 16 × 16 and NIRSport 8 × 8 systems (NIRx Medizintechnik GmbH, Berlin, Germany) operating in continuous wave mode. The setup included 24 sources (light emitters with wavelengths of 760 and 850 nm, 5 mW per wavelength) and 24 detectors (Si photodiode) positioned approximately 3 cm apart over the sensorimotor and adjacent areas. Multiple light sources were activated simultaneously, resulting in a sampling rate of 13.56 Hz. One of the detectors was found to be malfunctioning, resulting in the exclusion of channels formed by this detector and its symmetric counterparts in the opposite hemisphere. The remaining source-detector pairs provided 70 functional fNIRS channels.

EEG signals were recorded using an NVX-52 amplifier (MCS, Zelenograd, Russia) with 8 passive Ag/AgCl electrodes positioned according to the international 10/5 system at the following sites: ‘FCC3’, ‘FCC4’, ‘FCC5’, ‘FCC6’, ‘CCP3’, and ‘CCP4’ with ‘T9’ and ‘T10’ as reference. Electrode positions were chosen to be placed between the fNIRS optodes to cover regions over the sensorimotor cortices. Electrode impedances were kept below 10 kΩ. Electromyographic (EMG) activity was recorded from the m. extensor digitorum communis muscles of both hands using bipolar surface electrodes in a belly-tendon montage. Electrocardiogram (ECG) was recorded using bipolar electrodes positioned on the wrists of both hands (standard Einthoven lead I configuration). Thus, the EEG recording file contains 8 scalp EEG channels, two EMG channels (right and left hand), and one ECG channel. EMG and ECG signals were recorded using the same amplifier as the EEG. Data were digitized at a sampling rate of 500 Hz.

Data synchronization between EEG and fNIRS systems was achieved through a custom Arduino-based trigger system integrated with the visual stimulus presentation paradigm. At each visual stimulus onset, the controller simultaneously transmitted trigger pulses to the TTL synchronization inputs of both the EEG amplifier and fNIRS device. This hardware-level synchronization protocol ensured precise temporal synchronization of the signals with experimental events. Figure 2 illustrates the scalp positions of all light sources, detectors and EEG-channels at the scalp.Fig. 2
(A) (Left) Locations of all fNIRS and EEG sources, detectors, and channels on a scalp schematic using the standard 10/20 electrode system. (Right) Positions of fNIRS sources, detectors, and channels visualized on 3D brain models. (B) Sequence of fNIRS sources activation. (C) Sequence of visual stimuli presentation: ‘flash_l’ refers to the left LED flash, and ‘flash_r’ refers to the right LED flash.

sec.title: Data preprocessing
sec.p: Initially, files containing a large number of artefacts and incorrect triggers were excluded, resulting in the removal of 30 recordings (mainly from the first three patients).

For data preprocessing the Python library MNE-Python v1.3.1 was used15. The raw voltage data were converted into optical density values using the function “mne.preprocessing.nirs.optical_density(raw_intensity)”. The raw EEG signal was not preprocessed in any way.

sec.title: Data Records
sec.p: All the fNIRS, EEG, patient demographic profiles and clinical assessments data are available at Figshare16.

EEG and fNIRS data are provided in.fif format, compatible with the MNE Python library15. Additionally, fNIRS files are available in Shared Near Infrared Spectroscopy Format (SNIRF)17 and EEG files in European Data Format (EDF)18. The SNIRF files are provided with event files in the form of.txt tables containing arrays of event timestamps and the corresponding event codes. A python script for signal loading, pre-processing and epoching is provided with the dataset. Both EEG and fNIRS file names follow a standard structure. File names encode the subject ID, the side of the paretic limb (R for right, L for left), the session number (where session 1 indicates the first day of rehabilitation and session 6 is the last session before the patient was discharged) and the target hand involved in the motor task. For example, the file fNIRS/R_02_session2_right1 contains fNIRS data from Subject 2 - whose right hand is affected - recorded during the second session, with the first run containing right hand responses.

Each file contains channel position coordinates and metadata detailing the acquisition protocol and experimental events. Events are labelled according to the side of the visual stimulus: ‘flash_l’ indicates a left-side stimulus and ‘flash_r’ indicates a right-side stimulus (see Fig. 2C). Thus, in this file with right hand responses, ‘flash_l’ denotes non-target trials (no movement) and ‘flash_r’ corresponds to right hand movement trials.
fNIRS data are recorded as optical density values at two wavelengths (760 nm and 850 nm). EEG files contain raw EEG signals along with ECG and EMG channels, all recorded in volts.

sec.title: Spatio-temporal characteristics of hemodynamic response during impaired movements
sec.p:
Figure 3 shows data from Patient 03 with left hemisphere stroke during paretic hand movement. The topographic distribution of HbO concentration shows the evolution of the hemodynamic response across sensorimotor cortical regions bilaterally, with a more pronounced response in the contralesional (right) hemisphere. Maps represent the median of all experimental sessions for the patient.Fig. 3
(Upper) Topographic maps of HbO concentration dynamics at different time points during paretic right-hand movement in the Patient 03 with left hemisphere stroke. White vertical lines on the line plot indicate the corresponding time points post-stimulus. (Lower) Averaged hemodynamic response for HbO across right and left hemisphere channel groups (as shown in the scalp schematic on the right) during paretic hand movement. Shaded areas represent the 95% confidence interval.

The hemodynamic response was obtained by applying a bandpass filter (0.05–0.7 Hz) to the raw data, followed by epoching within the [−0.1–11 s] interval. The data were then converted to relative oxy- and deoxyhemoglobin concentrations (HbO and HbR, respectively) using the Beer-Lambert law with partial wavelength factors for each wavelength = 4. For visualization, signals in two channel clusters over the right and left sensorimotor areas were averaged. The resulting waveforms show initial activation in the ipsilesional (left) hemisphere contralateral to the responding hand, followed by subsequent response in the contralesional (ipsilateral) hemisphere. This temporal pattern of bilateral activation may indicate compensatory recruitment of contralesional sensorimotor networks during paretic hand movement.

sec.title: Spatio-temporal characteristics of EEG response during impaired movements
sec.p:
Figure 4 shows EEG data analysis results for the same patient. For this analysis, EEG signals were bandpass filtered (0.5–40 Hz) and segmented into epochs within the [−0.2–2 s] interval. Time–frequency analysis was performed using the Morlet wavelet transform. The frequencies of the wavelets ranged from 1 Hz to 30 Hz in 0.4 Hz step. The full-width at half-maximum was 270 ms. Movement-related event-related (de)synchronization (ERD/S) was computed as the logarithmic ratio of spectral power in motor-response epochs to baseline power, according to the formula: ERD/S = log₁₀(P_movement / P_baseline), where P_movement denotes the time–frequency power during target (motor-response) epochs, and baseline power P_baseline denotes the median power across non-target (no-response) epochs within the 1–4 s post-stimulus interval. The first second of the signal was excluded to avoid effects of evoked activity. Negative values indicate desynchronization, whereas positive values reflect synchronization. This approach has been validated for motor-related spectral perturbation analysis19–21.Fig. 4
(Upper) Alpha- and beta-ERD topographic distribution maps during paretic right-hand movement in the Patient 03 with left hemisphere stroke. (Middle) Dynamics of ERD/S in FCC3 (left) and FCC4 (right) electrodes. (Lower) Motor-related cortical responses after stimulus in FCC3 and FCC4 electrodes. Mean and 95% confidence intervals are shown. ERD/S and MRCP were obtained by averaging all epochs in all sessions of the patient.

To analyse the motor-related cortical potentials (MRCPs), the EEG signal was band-pass filtered within the 1–20 Hz frequency range and baseline-corrected using the −0.2 to −0.1 s pre-stimulus interval. Next, stimulus-locked averaging of the left- and right-hand response epochs was performed separately. This analysis captures early pre- and post-movement cortical potentials, reflecting the activation of the sensorimotor cortex at various stages of motor processing22.

ERD/S analysis revealed neural activation in the ipsilesional hemisphere (left hemisphere, intact) during affected hand movements: alpha (7–11 Hz) and beta (15–25 Hz) ERD developed after stimulus onset predominantly in FCC4 channel. Conversely, motor-related cortical potentials indicated the affected hemisphere to be more active in impaired-hand responses. Figure 3B shows MRCPs obtained from stimulus-locked averages of all target epochs. The negative component peaking at approximately 0.5 s after stimulus onset corresponds to the lateralized readiness potential (LRP), followed by a positive deflection representing the reafferent potential (RAP)23. Both LRP and RAP amplitudes were larger over channel FCC3.

As Figs. 3 and 4 demonstrate, the dataset enables analysis of fNIRS recordings for detecting cortical hemodynamic responses during motor tasks, as well as EEG recordings suitable for motor-related cortical potential and event-related desynchronization analysis. Thus, the provided multimodal data capture different aspects of sensorimotor cortical activation with complementary temporal and spatial characteristics.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study has several limitations, including a limited number of EEG channels, incomplete session coverage for some participants, and a limited sample size. At the same time, the multiple repetitions per patient increase the reliability of the data and allow analysis of rehabilitation-related dynamics over time.

sec.title: Usage Notes
sec.p:
This dataset includes multiple sessions of synchronously recorded multichannel fNIRS and EEG signals from post-stroke patients during motor rehabilitation. Few datasets exist that capture hemodynamic activity in stroke patients. Most are limited in the number of channels and capture activity only from a specific cortical region. The present dataset therefore offers unique opportunities to advance both basic neurophysiological research and stroke rehabilitation studies. For example:
The dataset includes recordings of movements from both the paretic and non-paretic hands, allowing the investigation of stroke-related effects not only on the impaired limb, but also on the function of the contralateral, presumably intact hand, a topic that remains underexplored in the literature.
The dataset contains multiple records for each patient before and during the rehabilitation period, allowing evaluation of cortical activity changes during the recovery period and matching them with sensorimotor skills restoration progress.
The multi-channel fNIRS setup allows reliable functional connectivity analysis across distributed cortical areas.
Simultaneous recording of fNIRS and EEG provides an opportunity to analyze neuro-vascular coupling processes.

fn.p: Publisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This work was performed within the scope of the State Assignment of the Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation, FZWM-2024-0013.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: A.M. and N.S. - conception and design of the experiment, data collection, the dataset preparation and technical validation, drafting of the manuscript, editing and revision of the manuscript, creating the illustrations. L.Y. - conception of the experiment, editing and revision of the manuscript, A.Y. - data collection, conducting clinical assessments, A.B.A. - the dataset preparation, editing and revision of the manuscript, NS - partial founding of the study, study coordination, G.I., A.K. - supervision.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: All the fNIRS, EEG data, patient demographic profiles and clinical assessments data are available at Figshare16. The Python scripts for data import, basic preprocessing and plotting the results are attached together with the data.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.

element-citation.article-title: New insights into old waves. Matching stimulus-and response-locked ERPs on the same time-window

citation-alternatives.mixed-citation: Berchicci, M., Spinelli, D. & Di Russo, F. New insights into old waves. Matching stimulus-and response-locked ERPs on the same time-window. Biological psychology117, 202–215, 10.1016/j.biopsycho.2016.04.007 (2016).27086274 10.1016/j.biopsycho.2016.04.007
